skill.name: judge
skill.description: Evaluates competing code implementations with a structured five-criterion scoring rubric for test-kitchen workflows.
skill.summary: Compare implementation variants with a repeatable scoring worksheet and winner selection rubric.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found several high-risk patterns, but review shows they are false positives from Markdown formatting, rubric text, and placeholder labels. No evidence found for command execution, credential theft, weak cryptography, Windows SAM access, reconnaissance, network use, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command finding at line 18 is a Markdown code fence that starts an output template. It does not instruct the model to run shell commands or execute backtick content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains only a Markdown fence marker for a formatted example. No command name, shell invocation, or user-controlled execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Windows SAM Database Pattern
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file findings at lines 100 and 103 are placeholder text inside a scoring template. They do not reference Windows paths, registry hives, hashes, or credential files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matching text is bracketed placeholder language for repeated sections. There is no semantic evidence of Windows SAM access or credential extraction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings are triggered by ordinary words in the description and rubric. The skill contains no hashing algorithm use, encryption code, or security-sensitive cryptographic guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines discuss implementation labels, design adherence, and scoring context. No cryptographic primitive or implementation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding at line 128 appears in a prompt placeholder asking for trade-offs between implementations. It does not request host, network, account, or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line asks what alternate implementations did better as part of a comparison rubric. It is unrelated to system discovery or operational reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Compare Code Implementations with a Judge Rubric
content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to choose between similar code implementations using consistent criteria. This skill provides a structured rubric for scoring, gates, trade-offs, and winner selection.
actual_capabilities: Scores implementations across fitness, complexity, readability, robustness, and maintainability.
Creates gate checks for test results and design adherence.
Applies hard gates for large fitness gaps and critical flaws.
Produces a scorecard that compares multiple implementation variants.
Summarizes winner rationale and trade-offs for rejected options.
limitations: It does not run tests or inspect files unless the host agent provides that context.
It depends on accurate implementation context and test results from earlier workflow phases.
It is designed for comparison workflows, not general code review alone.
It uses integer rubric scores and does not support weighted scoring.

use_cases.title: Select the best generated implementation
use_cases.description: Compare multiple generated code variants and choose the one that best satisfies the stated requirements.

use_cases.title: Review competing architecture approaches
use_cases.description: Score different implementation strategies against maintainability, robustness, and purpose fit before merging work.

use_cases.title: Document selection decisions
use_cases.description: Create a concise scorecard that explains why one implementation won and what trade-offs remain.

prompt_templates.title: Score two implementations
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner comparison after both implementations are in context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the judge rubric to compare impl-1 and impl-2. Include gate checks, criterion scores, total scores, winner, and trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate three variants
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard cookoff with three comparable implementations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Judge impl-1, impl-2, and impl-3 against the original requirements. Apply hard gates and explain the winning implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Compare different solution approaches
prompt_templates.scenario: Omakase workflow where variants may use different designs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the judge rubric for variant-a and variant-b. Treat fitness gaps as valid solution differences, then identify the best approach.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a close decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review when scores are similar and trade-offs matter.
prompt_templates.prompt: Re-score the candidate implementations with special attention to hidden complexity, future maintenance cost, and robustness under realistic load.

output_examples.input: Compare impl-1 and impl-2 for a small API refactor.
output_examples.output: A gate check table, a five-part scoring worksheet for each implementation, a total scorecard, hard gate results, and a winner rationale.

output_examples.input: Judge variant-a and variant-b for two different product designs.
output_examples.output: A rubric-based comparison that explains fitness differences, identifies feasibility flags, and records trade-offs from the non-winning variant.

best_practices: Provide original requirements, test results, and implementation summaries before invoking the skill.
Use the same evidence standard for every implementation being compared.
Record trade-offs even when one implementation clearly wins.
anti_patterns: Do not use it before implementation code and test outcomes are available.
Do not treat equal total scores as interchangeable without reviewing hard gates.
Do not skip rubric sections because one implementation appears obviously better.

faq.question: What does this skill evaluate?
faq.answer: It evaluates competing code implementations using five criteria: fitness, complexity, readability, robustness, and maintainability.

faq.question: Can it run tests?
faq.answer: No. It can use test results provided by the host agent, but it does not execute tests itself.

faq.question: How many implementations can it compare?
faq.answer: The template directly supports two or three implementations, with notes for optional third variants.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude?
faq.answer: No. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Does it support different design approaches?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for omakase-style comparisons where variants may use different solution designs.

faq.question: What output should users expect?
faq.answer: Users should expect a structured worksheet, scorecard, hard gate review, winner selection, and trade-off summary.
